summary:
Niccolò Machiavelli's The Prince (Il Principe, written 1513, published 1532 AD) was the most controversial and most influential work of political philosophy produced in the Renaissance — a short, clear treatise on how rulers acquire and maintain power that dispensed with medieval ideas of Christian kingship and introduced a frank, amoral analysis of political realities that gave the language a new adjective: 'Machiavellian.' Written by a Florentine civil servant who had observed the chaotic [[event:2492|Italian Wars]] at close range and been imprisoned and tortured by the Medici after their restoration, The Prince addressed a dedicatee (Lorenzo de' Medici) who would decide whether to use Machiavelli's expertise — making it simultaneously a job application and a genuine work of political science.

Machiavelli's core insight — that rulers must be willing to act immorally when necessary, that 'the ends justify the means,' and that appearing virtuous matters more than being virtuous — scandalized his contemporaries and subsequent generations. The book was placed on the Catholic Church's Index of Forbidden Books (1559) and inspired Shakespeare's Iago and other theatrical villains. However, more sympathetic readings note that Machiavelli was describing political reality rather than prescribing immorality — trying to equip rulers to actually protect their people rather than pursuing quixotic virtue at the cost of security. His Discourses on Livy, a longer work analyzing the Roman republic, showed his genuine admiration for republican self-government; The Prince represented a specific, crisis-driven analysis of princely power rather than his complete political vision. Machiavelli's insistence on analyzing politics empirically, based on historical observation rather than religious ideal, established him as a founder of modern political science.
